does: This paper introduces ONI, a distributed system for synthesizing dense intrinsic reward functions from LLM feedback to train RL agents without requiring dense external rewards. The core contribution combines an asynchronous LLM server that annotates agent trajectories with natural language evaluations and a learnable reward model that distills these annotations into a scalable reward function. The architecture decouples LLM inference from policy learning through distributed computation, enabling the approach to handle large-scale environments requiring billions of samples. The method explores different reward modeling strategies and demonstrates how online annotation of collected experience can provide dense reward signals for open-ended exploration and sparse-reward problems.
connects: This work extends recent efforts to leverage LLM priors for reward design in reinforcement learning, building on methods that use language models to provide implicit supervision for learning. It responds directly to limitations in prior approaches like LLM-based reward annotation that either require prohibitively expensive per-observation LLM calls or depend on pre-collected diverse offline datasets. The paper connects to classical RL work on intrinsic motivation and reward shaping, while combining modern insights about distilling knowledge from large models into smaller learned functions. It engages with hierarchical skill learning and open-ended exploration by enabling agents to receive interpretable feedback that guides discovery of useful behaviors beyond sparse environment rewards.
enables: This approach opens several research directions in scalable reward learning and autonomous agent training. By decoupling LLM annotation from policy learning through distributed systems, it enables investigation of how to efficiently use expensive language model compute for reward synthesis at scale. The work points toward more autonomous agent development where agents discover skills through interpretable, language-based feedback rather than hand-crafted rewards. It sets up future research into optimizing the annotation curriculum—determining which trajectories most benefit from LLM feedback—and exploring how reward models trained this way transfer across tasks or domains. The architecture could enable deployment of agents in complex, open-ended environments where defining reward functions is infeasible, advancing towards more general-purpose autonomous systems.
